guideline_source: Rwanda National Malaria Treatment Guidelines 2020, p.18
scenario: A child aged 3 years, weight 14kg, presents with fever (38.8°C), loss of appetite, and malaise for 1 day. RDT is positive for Plasmodium falciparum. No vomiting, no convulsions, and the child is alert. What is the recommended treatment?

ground_truth.dosage: 1.5 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days — weight band 10–14 kg (round to nearest practical dose; Rwanda protocol specifies 1 tablet for 5–14kg in some formulations — verify against current local supply). Ensure dose given with food or milk.
ground_truth.additional: Give paracetamol syrup for fever relief. Ensure caregiver observes first dose. Advise on re-presentation if vomiting within 30 minutes of dose.
ground_truth.referral: No referral. Manageable at health post level for uncomplicated malaria.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving adult dose (4 tablets) to a 14kg child; giving SP or quinine as first-line
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring to district hospital for uncomplicated RDT-positive malaria in a stable child
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Recommending any drug other than AL as first-line for uncomplicated P. falciparum

guideline_source: Rwanda National Malaria Treatment Guidelines 2020, p.28
scenario: An adult male, 65kg, is brought in by family. He is confused, has a very high fever (40.5°C), and cannot walk steadily. RDT is positive. He is breathing fast. The family says he has had symptoms for 3 days and has been getting worse. What do you do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: IV/IM Artesunate — pre-referral treatment before immediate referral
ground_truth.dosage: Artesunate 2.4mg/kg IV or IM at 0, 12, and 24 hours, then daily. If artesunate unavailable: IM quinine 20mg/kg loading dose. Give at CHW level as pre-referral ONLY — do not delay referral for repeated doses.
ground_truth.additional: Establish IV access. Check blood glucose (hypoglycaemia is common). Do not give oral medication to a confused patient. Position correctly to protect airway. Document and send referral note.
ground_truth.referral: IMMEDIATE referral to district hospital. Severe malaria is a medical emergency with >20% mortality if untreated. Pre-referral artesunate must be given but referral must not be delayed.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving oral AL to a confused patient; wrong artesunate dosing interval; not checking blood glucose
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Managing severe malaria entirely at CHW level; giving quinine oral tablets
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending ICU, mechanical ventilation, or dialysis at district level when these are unavailable

guideline_source: Rwanda National Tuberculosis Control Programme Guidelines 2021
scenario: A 28-year-old woman presents with a cough lasting 4 weeks, night sweats, and has lost 5kg over the past month. Sputum GeneXpert result confirms Mycobacterium tuberculosis, rifampicin-sensitive. She is HIV-positive (diagnosed 2 years ago, on ART). What treatment do you initiate?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Standard RNTCP regimen: 2HRZE/4HR — rifampicin-sensitive TB in HIV-positive patient
ground_truth.dosage: Intensive phase: 2 months HRZE (Isoniazid 5mg/kg + Rifampicin 10mg/kg + Pyrazinamide 25mg/kg + Ethambutol 15mg/kg daily). Continuation phase: 4 months HR. Use FDC tablets where available. Do not adjust ART without specialist guidance.
ground_truth.additional: Do NOT stop ART — continue and monitor for drug interactions (rifampicin induces CYP450, affects efavirenz levels). Refer to HIV/TB co-management clinic. Give pyridoxine 25mg daily to prevent isoniazid-induced neuropathy. Register in national TB system.
ground_truth.referral: Refer to TB/HIV integration clinic for co-management. Drug interaction review needed. No emergency referral required but co-management is standard of care.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Using 3-drug regimen; wrong duration; not giving pyridoxine
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Stopping ART to start TB treatment; not registering in TB programme; not testing contacts
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring patient to a tertiary hospital rather than the accessible TB/HIV integration clinic

guideline_source: Rwanda Ministry of Health Cholera Response Protocol 2022
scenario: A 40-year-old man is brought in severely dehydrated after 12 hours of profuse rice-water diarrhoea and vomiting. He is barely conscious, has no palpable peripheral pulse, and skin pinch returns very slowly. He cannot drink. What is the immediate treatment?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: IV Ringer's Lactate (preferred) or normal saline — rapid IV rehydration for severe dehydration
ground_truth.dosage: Adults: 100ml/kg Ringer's Lactate in 3 hours (30ml/kg in first 30 minutes, then 70ml/kg over 2.5 hours). Reassess every 30 minutes. Once can drink, switch to ORS. Doxycycline 300mg single dose when able to swallow.
ground_truth.additional: IMMEDIATE action. Establish 2 large-bore IV lines. Elevate legs. Monitor vitals every 15 minutes. Report outbreak to district health office IMMEDIATELY. Isolate and set up cholera cot. Disinfect environment.
ground_truth.referral: Manage at current facility if IV fluids available. Transfer if no IV capacity. Do not delay IV fluids for transfer.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Using oral ORS for a patient who is unconscious; wrong IV fluid rate; using dextrose instead of Ringer's Lactate
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not isolating patient; not reporting outbreak; giving oral fluids to unconscious patient
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending a CT scan, haematology workup, or laboratory investigations not relevant to acute cholera management

guideline_source: Rwanda National Sickle Cell Disease Management Guidelines 2020
scenario: A 6-year-old known sickle cell patient is brought in with a temperature of 38.6°C. The parent says the child has been feverish since this morning and seems more tired than usual. There is no obvious pain crisis. What is the clinical priority here, and what do you do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Fever in sickle cell disease is a medical emergency until proven otherwise — treat as potential sepsis
ground_truth.dosage: Do NOT wait to identify cause of fever. Give antipyretics (paracetamol 15mg/kg). Take blood culture and malaria RDT immediately. Give empirical antibiotic: amoxicillin-clavulanate or penicillin V per weight if no IV access. Refer for IV antibiotics if any concern.
ground_truth.additional: Children with SCD have functional asplenia and are highly susceptible to pneumococcal and other bacterial infections. Fever above 38.5°C in SCD requires same-day evaluation and often IV antibiotics.
ground_truth.referral: Refer to district hospital for IV antibiotics if: temperature >38.5°C, child appears unwell, or no oral antibiotic access. Do not send home with paracetamol alone.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving aspirin (contraindicated); underdosing paracetamol; not starting antibiotics empirically
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Treating as simple fever without recognising SCD-specific emergency; sending home on paracetamol only
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending bone marrow biopsy or haematology consult for acute fever — not the immediate priority

guideline_source: Rwanda EmONC National Guidelines 2020, Active Management of Third Stage Labour (AMTSL)
scenario: A woman has just delivered a healthy baby at your health centre. The baby is now on the mother's chest. What should you do in the next few minutes to prevent postpartum haemorrhage?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Active Management of Third Stage Labour (AMTSL): Oxytocin 10 IU IM within 1 minute of birth
ground_truth.dosage: Oxytocin 10 IU IM within 1 minute of delivery of the baby (before placenta delivers). Controlled cord traction after uterus contracts. Uterine massage after placenta delivery. Monitor for 2 hours post-delivery.
ground_truth.additional: If oxytocin unavailable: misoprostol 600mcg orally. Do not use ergometrine if hypertension suspected. Document time of oxytocin administration. Measure blood loss. Estimate fundal height.
ground_truth.referral: No referral needed for routine AMTSL. Refer only if PPH occurs despite AMTSL.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving oxytocin before delivery of baby (causes fetal distress); wrong dose; giving misoprostol rectally when oral route intended for prevention
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not giving oxytocin routinely; delayed cord clamping without following AMTSL protocol; not monitoring post-delivery
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring all deliveries to hospital for oxytocin administration

guideline_source: Rwanda National Guidelines for Maternal and Child Health 2020
scenario: A pregnant woman at 32 weeks gestation comes for her routine ANC visit. Her blood pressure reading today is 152/98 mmHg. Last visit (4 weeks ago) it was 118/76. She has a headache but no visual disturbances or epigastric pain. Urine dipstick shows 2+ protein. What do you do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: This presentation — BP >140/90 + proteinuria after 20 weeks — is pre-eclampsia. Immediate referral is required.
ground_truth.dosage: While preparing transfer: antihypertensive if BP >160/110 — labetalol 200mg oral or nifedipine 10mg sublingual. MgSO4 for seizure prophylaxis if at referral-capable facility (4g IV loading dose). Do not attempt full management at CHW/health post level.
ground_truth.additional: Lay patient on left side. Monitor BP every 15 minutes. Do NOT delay transfer. Call ahead to receiving facility. Document all findings. Do NOT give aspirin at this stage.
ground_truth.referral: URGENT referral to district hospital with obstetric capacity. Pre-eclampsia can progress to eclampsia rapidly. This is a life-threatening emergency.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving MgSO4 without resuscitation equipment available; giving nifedipine to someone already on labetalol without monitoring
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Managing pre-eclampsia at CHW level without referral; giving aspirin; telling patient to rest at home
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending specialists not available at district hospital (e.g. nephrology, maternal-fetal medicine) before initial stabilisation
